questions.question: Which road did the poet choose? [Poem: The Road Not Taken]
questions.explanation: The poet chooses the less travelled road.

questions.explanation: The roads represent the dilemma of decision-making in life.

questions.question: What does the poet mean by yellow wood? (Road not Taken)

options.option: Forest covered with yellow or dried leaves

questions.explanation: The ‘yellow woods’ signify a forest in the autumn season, covered with yellow or dried leaves.

questions.question: What has made all the difference in the poet’s life?
questions.explanation: Choosing a less travelled road has made all the difference for the poet.

questions.question: Road is a metaphor for
questions.explanation: 'Road' is a metaphor for the choices/decisions we make in life.

questions.question: Name the poet of the poem, The Road Not Taken.
questions.explanation: The poem, The Road Not Taken is written by American poet Robert Frost.

questions.question: What is the rhyme scheme of the poem The Road Not Taken?
questions.explanation: The rhyme scheme followed in the poem is abaab, cdccd, efeef, ghggh.

questions.question: In the poem, The Road Not Taken written by Robert Frost, Road has been used as a metaphor. What does the road represent?
questions.explanation: The idea that Frost expresses here is to travel on any one of the two roads, wherein the road becomes a metaphor for symbolising one's journey in life through the path that they choose to follow.

questions.question: What is the synonym for diverged?
questions.explanation: forked means separated into two or many divisions.

questions.question: What did the poet mean to express in the poem, The Road Not Taken by emphasising on his action of choosing one road over the other?

options.option: the choices that people make on the paths in a forest
the choices that people make when they are travelling
the choices that people make when returning home by a road
the choices that people make in the path of life

questions.explanation: The poem is a metaphor for the various choices that people make at certain points in their life which may lead to their sacrifice of something close to them or celebrating the path that they chose to walk on in life.

questions.question:
In the poem, The Road Not Taken What does the poet mean by the following line:
Oh, I kept the first for another day!

options.option: to travel the first road another day if he doesn't reach anywhere on the second
He would choose the other path of life some other day
to travel the first road another day to go to someplace else
he would need a map to travel the first road so he kept it for another day

questions.explanation: Standing at a point in life, he chose to take a path uncertain where it might lead him to but with a wish to return someday and walk down the other path, the would to travel both the paths and experience the variations that they present.

questions.question: The theme of the poem The Road Not Taken is set in which background?
questions.explanation: Two roads have diverged in a yellow wood and the poet walks through one of them.

questions.question: Which season does the poet suggest in the poem The Road Not Taken?
questions.explanation: The poem begins with the line Two roads diverged in a yellow wood where the word yellow, works as an adjective describing the wood. It suggests that the leaves of all the trees in the forest have turned yellow indicating the season of Autumn, popularly known as Fall in American English.

questions.question:
Which figure of speech has been implemented in the following line:
 Because it was grassy and wanted wear?
questions.explanation: The use of repeated sounds in consecutive or closely connected words is known as alliteration.

questions.question:
What does the poet mean by the following lines:
 I took the one less travelled by
 And that has made all the difference.

options.option: He took a different route and reached another place.
He made a tough choice in life leading to a different life.
The path he took was not much travelled by people so he got lost.
The road he took was less travelled and so he faced the deep woods that others don't come across.

questions.explanation: Going through a background study of the poet it can be said that the poet takes a different decision which is generally avoided by people and that has caused him to lead a different life than others. There are two possible interpretations of this poem wherein the poet may or may not be happy in the path he chose to live.

questions.question: What is the tone of the poem The Road Not Taken?
questions.explanation: The poem reflects on the choices that people make in their life and the outcome that they face.

questions.question: Why did the poet title his poem as The Road Not Taken?

options.option: because he thought it better to say about the road not taken.
because he regretted not having chosen the other road.
because he couldn't find a suitable title for his poem.

questions.explanation: The poet chose to walk down the path less trodden by which had probably led him to despair and suffering and he, therefore, regretted about the road that he had not taken that would presumably lead him to prosperity and happiness.

questions.question: In the poem, The Road Not Taken the poet says, "I shall be telling this with a sigh." Why do you think he uses the word sigh?

options.option: He is happy for the choice he has made

questions.explanation: The poem can have two possible interpretations. The first where the poet is happy for the path he chooses to follow in his life but the language used in the last stanza suggests the underlying regret in his voice for not having chosen the first road (second interpretation).

questions.question:
What do the following lines suggest in the context of the poem The Road Not Taken?
And both that morning equally lay
 In leaves, no step had trodden black

options.option: Both the roads had not been travelled that morning
The leaves on both the roads were all black in colour
The first road had been travelled but the second road lay new
Both the roads had been already travelled by people

questions.explanation: Metaphorically, it would mean that none of the choices was made for him by somebody else. In the context of the poem, no step had trodden black refers to the fact that none of the roads had been travelled that morning hence no leaves were marked black by the footsteps of other people.

subtopics.name: Beehive Poem A Slumber Did My Spirit Seal

questions.question: Who is the poet of the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: The poem is written by an English romantic poet, William Wordsworth in the year 1978.

questions.question:
Which figure of speech is used in the given line?
A slumber did my spirit seal.
questions.explanation: Alliteration refers to the use of the same letter or sound at the beginning of words that are closed together.

questions.question: What does the word slumber mean in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: The word Slumber means deep sleep in the poem.

questions.question: What is the rhyming scheme of the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: This rhyming scheme matches the phonetic patterns followed by the ending words of each line.

questions.question: What is the theme of the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: The poem is about the death of a loved one. The agony and trauma that one suffers from the sudden death of a loved one, shake our soul and mind. The poet beautifully expresses his pain and eternal love for his beloved and accepts that death is inevitable.

questions.question: What is the reason behind the poet's pensive mood in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: A poet is full of grief and sorrow as he has lost his beloved. Her sudden death has shaken him.

questions.question: How did the poet react to the death of his beloved in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?

options.option: He was in a remorseful state of mind

questions.explanation: The death of his beloved had shaken the poet deeply. He was in deep despair and remorse gripped him firmly. Moreover, he repented at his casual attitude towards life and death.

questions.question: How has the death of the poet's beloved been described in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: The sudden death of his beloved shattered the poet initially but soon he accepted the reality that death is bound to happen. No one is immortal and we all will become an integral part of nature someday.

questions.question: What was the fate of the poet's beloved in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?

options.option: She was in a poor state of health

questions.explanation: The poet's beloved died suddenly and unexpectedly. Her death left the poet traumatized and in a lamenting state of mind.

questions.question: How did the poet's beloved become an inseparable part of nature in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: The poet's beloved died and she was buried. The poet insists that after death the body becomes an integral part of the earth and it mixes with the layers of rocks and sand buried beneath the surface of the earth.

questions.question: Why is the poet's beloved unaffected by time?
questions.explanation: After death, time is just a quantity. It's of no significance to the dead and they won't age with the passing time.

questions.question: In what way does the poet imagine his beloved after her demise in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: The poet states that ultimately the corpses assimilate into the layers of rocks and sand buried deep inside the earth's crust and become an inseparable part of the earth.

questions.question: Who is devoid of human fears in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: The poet after the death of his beloved has accepted the universal truth that death is inevitable. He, now, is devoid of any common humanly fear that constantly chain our souls.

questions.question: What does the phrase earth's diurnal course mean?

options.option: Rotation of earth on it's own axis
Change in the temperature during a day

questions.explanation: The earth rotates on its own axis causing the transition between day and night.

questions.question: Who became a part of earth's diurnal course in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: The poet's beloved had died and was buried. The poet insisted that his beloved was now an inseparable part of the earth.

questions.question: What has led to the separation of the poet and his beloved in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?

options.option: Disloyal behavior on part of the poet

questions.explanation: The sudden and unexpected death of the poet's beloved separated the two souls and left the poet in a lamenting state of mind.

questions.question:
Who is lying motionless bin in the given line? 
No motion has she now, no force.
questions.explanation: The poet's beloved is lying without any energy and movement. She is lifeless and cannot interact with her surroundings.

questions.question:
Why is she not able to listen or see anything in the given line? 
No motion has she now, no force.
questions.explanation: Poet's beloved is lying still, without any movement and energy for anything as she is lifeless. She is devoid of all her senses.

questions.question:
Which human fears constantly pester mankind in the given line? 
No motion has she now, no force,
questions.explanation: These are the common worldly fears that have chained our souls.

questions.question: What does the phrase earthly years refer to in the poem, A slumber did my spirit seal?
questions.explanation: Earthly years refer to the years that have passed on earth. The poet uses his poetic liberty to refer to time as earthly years.

questions.question: What is the theme of the story The Lost Child?

options.option: The universality of a child’s desire for everything.
Fear of being lost as a child.

questions.explanation: The story concentrates on the universality of a child’s desire to get everything. It’s a story about a child’s aspirations, demands, and realization.

questions.question: How was the child lost in the fair?

options.option: His parents left him on the way.
He was attracted by many charming things on the way and stood at one place.
He got separated from his parents due to the heavy crowd in the fair.
He was angry with his parents and left them on the way.

questions.explanation: He was attracted by many charming things on the way and stood at one place and went on looking at things.

questions.question: How did the crowd manage to reach the fair in the plot The lost child?
questions.explanation: The excited crowd reached the fair by various means, some were pedestrians, some were on the horses and others traveled by carts.

questions.question: Who accompanied the little boy to the fair in The lost child?
questions.explanation: The child went to the fair with his parents.

questions.question: What was the child's favorite sweet in the story The lost child?
questions.explanation: The little boy's mouth watered as he spotted his favourite burfi at the sweetmeat countertop at the fair. He savoured to eat the sweet.

questions.question: What captured the little boy's attention at the flower shop?
questions.explanation: The child was irresistibly drawn towards a garland made of the flamboyant Gulmohar flowers kept at a flower counter at the fair.

questions.question: Who played the flute at the fair in The Lost Child?
questions.explanation: The child was mesmerized by the music from a flute played by a snake charmer at the fair. He stood playing the flute to a snake sitting inside a basket.

questions.question: What fell on the child as he entered the grove in the story The Lost Child?
questions.explanation: The little boy was delighted to gather the raining petals in his hands which fell accidentally upon the child as he entered the grove.

questions.question: How did the father of the little boy react when he asked for a toy in the story The Lost Child?

options.option: He hugged the child and ignored his demand.
He smiled and got the child a toy.
He got angry and refused to buy a toy.

questions.explanation: The father glared at him angrily in his familiar tyrant's way.

questions.question: What was the child's mother's reaction when he pleaded for a toy in the story The Lost Child?
questions.explanation: The mother's heart melted to the little child's plea but she tried to divert his attention from the toy's counter as she too was not willing to buy him the toy.

questions.question: What was compared to melting gold in the story The lost child?

options.option: The bright coloured clothes worn by the crowd
The yellow turban worn by the child

questions.explanation: The child's mother compared the flowering mustard-field to melting gold. She tried to divert her son's attention towards the field which appeared as pale as melting gold to her.

questions.question: How did the child feel on seeing the fair in the story The lost child?
questions.explanation: The child was delighted to see the fair but at the same time, he got puzzled and slightly petrified on seeing a huge crowd of people at the fair. He was irresistibly drawn towards stalls of toys, sweets, flowers, and a lot more.

questions.question: How did the child react when he realized that he was lost?
questions.explanation: Unable to spot his parents; the child got scared and he panicked. He frantically searched for them at the fair and on not being able to locate them, he started crying inconsolably.

questions.question: What was the child watching when he realized that he was lost?
questions.explanation: The child was fascinated by watching the roundabout at the fair. He desired to have a ride on the full swing roundabout as he gazed at the mob being carried away in a whirling motion on the ride.

questions.question: Who rescued the lost child?
questions.explanation: A man noticed the little boy caught in the crowd, he lifted the wailing child, tried to pacify him and did his best to unite him with his parents.

questions.question: Why did the little boy decide not to listen to the music played by the snake charmer?
questions.explanation: The child was mesmerized by the music played by the snake charmer but he chose to proceed farther as his parents had forbidden him to hear such coarse music.

questions.question: Where was the child spotted by the stranger in the story The Lost Child?
questions.explanation: The little child after being separated from his parents at the fair searched for them frantically and ran towards a shrine. A man noticed the wailing child and saved him from being trampled underfoot. His shrieks invited the kind man's attention.

questions.question: What was the child's reaction when the stranger offered him to buy toys and sweets?
questions.explanation: The little boy lost his interest in balloons, toys, sweets, and all other things that captured his mind previously. All he desired was, to reunite with his lost parents.

questions.question: Which festival was being celebrated in the story The lost child?
questions.explanation: The story was set in the spring season where people after a long and harsh winter enjoyed the onset of a pleasant season.

questions.question: What does the phrase gaily clad humanity mean in the plot The Lost child?
questions.explanation: The mob in the plot wore bright coloured clothes to mark the celebration of the spring festival. Vibrant colours were used to depict cheerfulness.

options.option: The child said, "I want my father, I want my mother."
The child said, "I want to go home."
The child said, "I live in a village."

questions.explanation: The child said, "I want my father, I want my mother."

questions.question: Who was Toto?
questions.explanation: Toto was a pretty monkey. Grandfather decided to add Toto to his private zoo.

questions.question: What kind of an animal was Toto?
questions.explanation: Toto was a troublemaker. He was a pretty and naughty monkey.

questions.question: Who brought Toto to the narrator's house?
questions.explanation: Toto was purchased by the writer's grandfather as he decided to add the little animal to his private zoo.

questions.question: Who sold Toto to the narrator's grandfather?
questions.explanation: The writer's grandfather purchased Toto from a tonga-driver. The driver used to keep the little animal tied to a feeding trough.

questions.question: What complimented Toto's good looks?
questions.explanation: Toto's tail not only enhanced its appearance but also served as a third hand. The tail facilitated the baby monkey to grab things and hang from branches.

questions.question: Who was expected to create a fuss over Toto's entry into the narrator's house?
questions.explanation: The grandmother always created a fuss when the narrator's grandfather brought home some new animal or bird. So, Toto was kept secretly in the house.

questions.question: What was the color of Toto?
Where did the writer's grandfather travel with Toto?
questions.explanation: The writer's grandfather had to leave for Saharanpur to collect his pension. So, he decided to take the monkey with him as Toto's presence was still a secret in the house.

questions.question: How was Toto carried on the train?

options.option: It sat on the seat beside the grandfather.
It was kept in a big black canvas kit bag.

questions.explanation: A big black canvas kit-bag was provided for Toto. The bag once closed had no escape and Toto comfortably sat on the straw which was spread at the bottom of the bag.

questions.question: Who objected to Toto's free travel on the train?
questions.explanation: The ticket collector noticed Toto at the railway turnstile, while the grandfather was producing his ticket and Toto suddenly poked it's head out of the canvas bag and gave the ticket collector a wide grin.

questions.question: How much fare was collected by the ticket collector for Toto's travel?
questions.explanation: Toto was classified as a dog by the ticket-collector and he charged three rupees from the narrator's grandfather.

questions.question: What did Toto throw at the narrator's grandmother?
questions.explanation: The grandmother yelled at Toto for consuming Pullao from a large dish kept on the table. So, the mischievous monkey out of rage threw a plate kept on the table at her.

questions.question: How did Toto react after throwing the dish down from the tree?
questions.explanation: Toto was extremely naughty and to avenge the narrator's grandmother who screamed at it, the monkey threw the dish down from the tree, and chattered with delight when it broke to the hundred pieces.

questions.question: Which other animal accompanied narrator's grandfather and Toto to Saharanpur?
questions.explanation: The grandfather was carrying a small tortoise in his pocket while travelling to Saharanpur.

questions.question: How did Toto take a bath in winter?

options.option: It took a warm water shower in the bathroom.
It was given a large bowl of warm water by the grandmother.
It was taken to the nearby river.
It skipped bathing due to harsh weather conditions.

questions.explanation: Toto enjoyed bathing with warm water given to it during winters. It used its hands to test the water temperature and then gradually stepped into the bath.

questions.question: Who was smashed by a glass of water on the face by Toto in the plot The Adventures of Toto?
questions.explanation: When the narrator's grandmother yelled at Toto for gobbling a delicacy kept on the table, the mischievous monkey threw a plate at her and one of the narrator's aunt received a glass of water on the face as she rushed forward.

questions.question: Why was Toto sold by the narrator's grandfather?
questions.explanation: The monkey was difficult to handle. The narrator's middle-class family could not bear the frequent damages of utensils and other household items that Toto caused.

questions.question: Who was Iswaran?
questions.explanation: Iswaran was a cook at Mahendra’s place. He was an asset for his master.

questions.question: What did Mahendra call the ghosts and spirits to be?
questions.explanation: Mahendra was a very practical man. He often chided Ishwaran not to believe in ghosts or spirits. He calls ghosts and spirits a figment of the imagination as he did not believe in ghosts.

questions.question: What do you think is the message or theme of the story Iswaran the Storyteller?

options.option: Everyone should know the art of story-telling
Ghost stories make us realise the real fear

questions.explanation: The basic theme of the story is that - nobody is free from fears - as we all accept the presence of ghosts and spirits, but we feel hesitant to express this truth openly. But that does not mean we should lead a fearful life, rather we should try to know the proper cause of our fear and learn to deal with it.

questions.question: What the author of the story Iswaran the Storyteller R.K Laxman famous for?
questions.explanation: R.K. Laxman is a renowned cartoonist of India. He is famous for his political cartoons and illustrations.

questions.question: Who is the author of the story Iswaran the Storyteller?
questions.explanation: The story has been written by the well-known Indian cartoonist R.K. Laxman.

questions.question: What was Mahendra's occupation in the story, Iswaran the Storyteller?
questions.explanation: Mahendra was a bachelor and worked as a Junior Supervisor in a firm which offered to hire supervisors for various construction sites like factories, bridges, dams and so on.

questions.question: What was Iswaran's occupation in the story, Iswaran the Storyteller?
questions.explanation: Mahendra had to travel from one place to another for work but his most important asset was Iswaran, his cook.

questions.question: Who told the story to Ganesh? (Iswaran the Storyteller)
questions.explanation: The story was told to Ganesh by Mahendra.

questions.question: Why do you think the author titled the story as Iswaran the Storyteller instead of naming it after Mahendra and his experiences?

options.option: Because Iswaran wanted the story to be named after him
Because there was a person named Iswaran
Because the story revolves around Iswaran and his stories

questions.explanation: The author could have titled his story after Mahendra and his experiences but the story actually revolves around Iswaran and the stories that he used to tell Mahendra.

questions.question: What did Iswaran use to see at night in the factory area? (Iswaran the Storyteller)
questions.explanation: Iswaran told Mahendra that he often saw ghosts at night because the place that they were living, was a burial ground.

questions.question: Why did Mahendra enjoy listening to Iswaran's stories? (Iswaran the Storyteller)

options.option: Because the stories were written by famous authors

questions.explanation: Mahendra liked listening to Iswaran's stories because of his inimitable style with which he told the stories.

questions.question:
Who said the following lines? (Iswaran the Storyteller)

It has something to do with Japanese art, I think, sir. Karate or ju-jitsu it is called. I had read about it somewhere. It temporarily paralyses the nervous system, you see.
questions.explanation: Iswaran told Mahendra about his brave act of defeating an elephant. Mahendra asked him how he managed to do such a thing to which Iswaran replied, "It has something to do with Japanese art, I think, sir. Karate or ju-jitsu it is called. I had read about it somewhere. It temporarily paralyses the nervous system, you see."

questions.question: What disturbed Iswaran the most at the factory area? (Iswaran the Storyteller)

options.option: A female ghost with a matted hair and a shrivelled face

questions.explanation: According to Iswaran, there was a female ghost who always appeared on and off during full moon nights. She was an ugly creature with matted hair and a shrivelled face. This disturbed Iswaran the most.

questions.question: Why is Iswaran referred to as the storyteller? (Iswaran the Storyteller)

options.option: Because he performed as a storyteller in various shows
Because he belonged to a family of storytellers
Because he was the one who told stories to Mahendra

questions.explanation: R.K. Laxman referred to Iswaran as the storyteller because he used to tell stories to Mahendra related to various topics from experiences to facts and because it was his stories that kept Mahendra entertained when he was off work.

questions.question: What seemed to be Iswaran's amazing speciality? (Iswaran the Storyteller)

options.option: To produce vegetables and cooking ingredients out of nowhere

questions.explanation: The author wrote in the story that Iswaran had an amazing capability to produce vegetables and cooking ingredients, seemingly out of nowhere.

questions.question: What did Mahendra see on a full moon night through the window of his room? (Iswaran the Storyteller)

options.option: A dark cloudy form clutching a bundle

questions.explanation: Mahendra was awakened by a sound of moan in the middle of full moon night. After much contemplation, he looked out of the window and was aghast to see a dark cloudy form, not far away from the window, clutching a bundle.

questions.question: Why was Mahendra determined to leave the place as soon as possible? (Iswaran the Storyteller)

options.option: Because he wanted to go back to his parents
Because he was posted to another place
Because he wanted to visit a nearby place

questions.explanation: Mahendra was determined to leave the haunted place as soon as possible after Iswaran confirmed what he saw last night to be true.

questions.question: Iswaran's stories and descriptions were influenced by which authors?  (Iswaran the Storyteller)
questions.explanation: The imaginative descriptions and the narrative flourishes of the popular Tamil thrillers enthralled him. His own descriptions and stories were hence, highly influenced by the Tamil authors.

questions.question: What kind of a place had the factory area once been according to Iswaran? (Iswaran the Storyteller)
questions.explanation: One day after dinner, Iswaran unexpectedly launched into a most garish account involving the supernatural. He told Mahendra that the factory ground which they were living in was once a burial ground.

questions.question: Why did Mahendra think Iswaran had gone crazy?

options.option: Because Iswaran spoke in a strange manner
Because Iswaran behaved in a strange manner
Because Iswaran said there was a ghost in the room
Because Iswaran told him about the ghosts that he saw there

questions.explanation: Iswaran narrated to Mahendra his encounters with the ghosts that roamed around the place they lived in and how he was disturbed by a female ghost that appeared on full moon nights. Mahendra did not believe Iswaran and thought he had gone crazy.

questions.question: How did the guru save his disciple in In the Kingdom of Fools?

options.option: Guru offered a handsome price to rescue his disciple.
He tempted the kingdom to a bounty.
Guru created a mystery and expressed his desire to die first.

questions.explanation: The guru created a mystery and told the king about his desire to die first. When the king asked for the reason for doing so. The guru told that it was the stake of God’s justice, whoever will claim first shall become the king in future.

questions.question: The folktale titled In the Kingdom of Fools has been taken from which author's collection of Folktales from India?
questions.explanation: The Kannada folktale has been taken from A.K. Ramanujan's Folktales of India.

questions.question: What did the king and the minister decide to alter in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: The king and the minister decided to alter day into night and night into day.

questions.question: Who were the two idiots in the kingdom? (In the Kingdom of Fools)
questions.explanation: Both the king and the minister in the Kingdom of Fools were idiots.

questions.question: Why did the disciple want to continue his stay at the Kingdom of Fools in spite of his guru's repetitive advice to leave the place in the text, In the Kingdom of Fools?

options.option: Because the place had a comfortable climate
Because everything was available at a cheap rate
Because the house they lived in was beautiful and he didn't want to leave it

questions.explanation: The disciple was only concerned with food and since it was available at a very cheap rate in the kingdom, he didn't want to leave the place.

questions.question: What was deemed as a punishable offence in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?

options.option: Disobeying the king's orders of work during night and sleep during day

questions.explanation: The king and the minister wanted to do things differently in their kingdom. So they ordered that people should be awake and go about their business at night and sleep during the day. Disobeying these orders of the king equalled to a punishable offence.

questions.question: Who became the new king and the new minister after the existing king and the minister were executed in the story, Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: After the execution of the king and the minister, the people of the kingdom pleaded the guru and his disciple to become the king and the minister of the kingdom. Hence, the guru became the new king with his disciple as the minister.

questions.question: Whom did the burglar's brother blame for the burglar's death in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: The burglar's brother rushed to the king to complain about the merchant. He blamed him for his brother's death because a wall of the merchant's house broke on the burglar's head killing him instantly on the spot while he was carrying out his ancient trade.

questions.question: What was the cost of every product that was available at the kingdom's market In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: The guru and the disciple were astonished to find that everything in the market was available at the same cost, a Duddu.

questions.question: Why do you think the story In the Kingdom of Fools has been titled so?

options.option: Because it talks about the happenings in a kingdom ruled by two fools
Because three fools lived in the kingdom
Because a group of fools travelled the kingdom

questions.explanation: The story has been aptly titled because it talks about the happenings that take place in a kingdom ruled by two fools.

questions.question: How many people were involved in the blame-game that took place at the king's justice court in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: Five people were involved in the blame-game beginning with the thief's brother who blamed the rich merchant for his brother's death and who instead blamed it on the poor bricklayer who built such a weak wall. The bricklayer accused the dancing girl, now an older woman for it. The older woman said it was not her fault, but the goldsmith's who made her walk to and fro. The goldsmith laid it all on the rich merchant again.

questions.question: Whom did the bricklayer blame for his inaccurate work in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: The bricklayer blamed the dancing girl for his inaccurate work because it was her ankles that jingled when she walked up and down the street which kept the bricklayer distracted from his work.

questions.question: What explanation did the guru give to the king for his wish to die first on the new stake in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?

options.option: He wanted to die before his disciple
The first person who dies on the new stake would be reborn as the king of the kingdom
The stake was new and should be used first on a wise man
He would be reborn as the minister of the kingdom if he dies first

questions.explanation: The Guru arrived on time to save his disciple and created a drama. He told the king that it was not an ordinary stake and whoever went to the stake first would become the king in next life. The second to die would become his minister.

questions.question: Why did the king decide to die first in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?

options.option: To take birth as the minister of the kingdom
To take birth again as the king of the kingdom

questions.explanation: A fool that he was, the king believed the guru's words and decided to die first followed by his minister so that they would born again as the king and the minister of the kingdom.

questions.question: Whom did the merchant blame for building a weak wall in his house in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: The merchant blamed the bricklayer for building a weak wall in his house.

questions.question: Whom did the goldsmith blame for having made the dancing girl walk up and down the street for a dozen times in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: The goldsmith blamed the merchant for having made the dancing girl walk up and down the street a dozen times because he could not make her jewellery on time. He was making the jewellery ordered by the merchant's family as they had a wedding and did not want to wait.

questions.question: Why were the two strangers amazed as they wandered about the town in the broad daylight? (In the Kingdom of Fools)

options.option: Because there was no food store in the city
Because everyone was sleeping in their houses during the day
Because the kingdom did not have any people and the houses were empty
Because the kingdom was ruled by a queen

questions.explanation: The guru and his disciple arrived at the Kingdom of Fools in the broad daylight but were amazed as they wandered about in the town. There was no one to be seen on the road during the day as everyone was fast asleep in their houses.

questions.question: Find the synonym of Lawman from the story, In the Kingdom of the Fools.
questions.explanation: The term lawman is synonymous to the term bailiff which represents a law officer who makes sure that the decisions taken by the authority or in a court are obeyed properly.

questions.question: Who is the wisest man in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: Guru was a wise man. He tried to warn his disciple about the dangers of staying in the kingdom of Fools.

questions.question: What leads the disciple towards danger in the story, In the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: His love for food made the disciple a fat man but led him to grave danger as the king had ordered his guards to bring a fat man who would fit the stake and he was dragged by the king's guards to the place of execution.

Because his statue was covered with gold, sapphire, and ruby.
Because he sees all the misery of the city.
Because he is no more alive now.

questions.explanation: The statue of the happy prince is not happy as he sees all the misery of his people and weeps to see them in trouble. He wanted to help them.

questions.explanation: The Happy Prince is an eye-opening short story in many ways written by Irish poet, author and playwright Oscar Wilde.

questions.explanation: One night a little swallow flew over the city of the Happy Prince, he was travelling to Egypt and needed a place to rest so he took shelter under the Prince's statue.

questions.question: What was the Prince gilded over with In the story, The Happy Prince?
questions.explanation: The Happy Prince was gilded all over with gold.

questions.question: Where was the swallow going In the story, The Happy Prince?
questions.explanation: The swallow was flying to Egypt to meet the rest of the birds of his kind but the Happy Prince stopped him from going since he wanted the Swallow to help him in ending the sufferings of others living in his city.

questions.question: What did the Prince tell the swallow to give to the seamstress In the story, The Happy Prince?

options.option: The thin leaves of gold from his body

questions.explanation: The seamstress was penniless and didn't have any money to buy oranges for her son, who was very ill. She was left only with river water. Looking at the helpless state of the seamstress,  the Prince told the swallow to give her the ruby from his sword's hilt.

questions.question: What were the Prince's eyes made up of in the story The Happy Prince?
questions.explanation: The Happy Prince's eyes were made of rare sapphires brought from India thousands of years ago.

questions.question: What did the Prince tell the swallow to give to the young writer? (The Happy Prince)

options.option: The ruby from the hilt of the Prince's sword

questions.explanation: The Prince told the swallow that his eyes were made of rare sapphires brought from India thousands of years ago. The swallow should give the writer one of the Prince's eye so that he could buy some firewood and finish writing his play.

questions.question: What did the Prince tell the swallow to give to the poor people in his city In the story, The Happy Prince?

options.option: The ruby from the hilt of his sword
The leaves of gold from his own self

questions.explanation: The Prince told the little swallow that he was covered with fine gold and urged the swallow to give all the gold leaves to the poor people of his city.

options.option: The young man who is a writer

questions.explanation: The swallow acted as a messenger for the Happy Prince as he carried out his orders and informed him about his city.

questions.question: What was the heart of the Happy Prince made up of in the story, The Happy Prince?
questions.explanation: The Happy Prince had a leaden heart as he was a statue built after the Happy Prince who once lived in the Palace.

options.option: Because he wanted to be called as the Happy Prince
Because he lived a happy life and died a happy death
Because the Prince had been named so by his parents

questions.explanation: The Happy Prince had always been happy because he lived in the Palace where sorrow was not allowed. He had lived a happy life and he died happy also.

questions.question: Who called the Prince as the Happy Prince when he was alive in the story, The Happy Prince?
questions.explanation: The Prince told the little swallow that when he was alive he did not know what tears were because he lived in a palace where sorrow was not allowed to enter. His courtiers used to call him the Happy Prince.

questions.question:
Who said the following lines in the story, The Happy Prince?
As he is no longer beautiful he is no longer useful.
questions.explanation: The Art Professor at the University, looking at the condition of the statue of the Happy Prince said, 'As he is no longer beautiful he is no longer useful'.

questions.question: Why do you think Wilde titled the story as, The Happy Prince?

options.option: Because he saw a happy prince while writing the story
Because the story is about the Happy Prince and his deeds after his death
Because he was suggested to title his story as The Happy Prince
Because he was writing the story for the Happy Prince

questions.explanation: The story talks about how the Happy Prince helped the people of his city after his death and how he gave them everything he had.

questions.question: What cracked inside the statue of the Happy Prince as the little swallow fell at his feet without life in the story, The Happy Prince?
questions.explanation: As the little swallow fell dead at his feet, the leaden heart of the Prince cracked into two.

questions.question: Who noticed the dull state of the Prince's statue in the story, The Happy Prince?

questions.explanation: When the Happy Prince was alive, he did not know what tears and sorrow were. After his death, he could see the ugliness and misery of the city. Therefore, he wept and was not really happy.

questions.question: What did God say to one of His Angels in the story, The Happy Prince?

options.option: To bring Him the most precious flower in the city
To bring Him the most precious thing from the city
To bring Him the two most precious things in the city
To bring Him the three most precious things in the city

questions.explanation: In the story, Oscar Wilde wrote about God's command to one of his Angels. He wished to have the two most precious things in the city and commands one of his Angels to bear the responsibility.

Who is the author of the prose, The Last Leaf?
questions.explanation: The Last Leaf is a heart-warming and inspiring short story written by the very famous American short-story writer O. Henry.

questions.question: Name the two friends from the story, The Last Leaf.
questions.explanation: The story begins with an introduction of the two friends and young artists, Sue and Johnsy who live together in a small flat.

questions.question: In the story The Last Leaf, who was Mr Behrman?
questions.explanation: Mr Behrman was a sixty-year-old painter whose lifelong dream was to paint a masterpiece.

questions.question: In the short story The Last Leaf,  who had painted the last leaf of the ivy creeper?
questions.explanation: The last leaf of the ivy creeper that never fell off in spite of tremendous storms and downpour was Mr Behrman's masterpiece that he had painted on one of the stormy nights for Johnsy.

questions.question: What did the last leaf of the ivy creeper represent for Johnsy in the story, The Last Leaf?

options.option: The leaf that she wanted to paint

questions.explanation: The last leaf of the ivy creeper was for Johnsy, her last breath of life because she felt that with the falling of the last leaf she would also pass away.

questions.question: In the story, The Last Leaf which disease was Johnsy suffering from?
questions.explanation: Johnsy had fallen seriously ill in the month of November as she was suffering from pneumonia.

questions.question: The story titled, The Last Leaf is set in which season of the year?
questions.explanation: The story is set in Autumn, the wind is blowing hard and it is raining heavily.

questions.question: In the story The Last Leaf, why was Johnsy counting the leaves of the ivy creeper?

options.option: To count the days for her approaching death
To see how many leaves were there in the plant altogether
She was amused to see so many leaves and so she counted them to remember

questions.explanation: Johnsy had given up all hope on life and strongly believed that she would die soon. She was counting the leaves of the ivy creeper in order to count the days that would take for her to die because she felt with the last leaf of the creeper gone she would also fall asleep forever.

questions.question: Why do you think Henry titled his short story like The Last Leaf?

options.option: Because there was the last leaf in a plant he had
Because the last leaf forms the main theme of the story
Because Henry liked the title very much
Because there was only one leaf in the ivy creeper

questions.explanation: The story shows how Johnsy had based her life on the last leaf and the way it transformed her life. It also portrays the fulfilment of dreams as in the case of Mr Behrman who dreamt of painting a masterpiece his whole, and before his death could finally paint this priceless masterpiece and fulfil his dream.

questions.question: In the story The Last Leaf, what does the last leaf actually symbolise?
questions.explanation: The Last Leaf is a symbol of inspiration to begin life anew, to dream of achieving success and joy by overcoming the adversities in life without falling prey to the hurdles that come in life.

questions.question:
In the story The Last Leaf, who said the following lines?
Johnsy it seems, has made up her mind that she is not going to get well.
questions.explanation: The doctor was worried because Johnsy was not responding to any medication and it seemed to him that she had given up on life and didn't want to live anymore.

questions.question: In the story, The Last Leaf who else was suffering from pneumonia other than Johnsy?
questions.explanation: Mr Behrman had taken great effort in painting the last leaf on Johnsy's window during the stormy night and was all wet in the rain. Due to his old age and the rain he too got affected with pneumonia but passed away in a very short period of time.

questions.question: In which month did Johnsy fall seriously ill with pneumonia according to the story, The Last Leaf?
questions.explanation: During autumn, Johnsy fell seriously ill in November, suffering from pneumonia.

questions.question: In the story The Last Leaf, when did Mr Behrman paint the leaf?

options.option: During the day when Johnsy was sleeping
In a stormy night when three leaves remained on the creeper
When Johnsy went to see the doctor
When the last leaf fell on a stormy night

questions.explanation: Mr Behrman had left without saying a word to Sue when he heard about Johnsy's condition. He was probably determined to somehow revive hope and inspiration in Johnsy's mind,  so he went out in the night during the storm and the rain to paint a leaf that would be alive forever when the last leaf of the creeper fell off during the storm.

questions.question: In the story The Last Leaf, who found Mr Behrman on the bed with his clothes and shoes wet and him shivering?
questions.explanation: Sue told Johnsy that the Janitor had found him on a bed with all his clothes and shoes wet and saw that he was shivering which explained that he had gone out on that stormy night.

questions.question: Find the word from the story, The Last Leaf which means a person whose job is to look after a building.
questions.explanation: The janitor looks after the building where he works, he can be referred to as a caretaker of a building.

questions.question: In the text The Last Leaf, on which storey of the old house was Sue and Johnsy's flat located?
questions.explanation: The small flat that Sue and Johnsy lived in was on the third storey of an old house.

questions.question: How many characters are there in the story, The Last Leaf by O. Henry?
questions.explanation: Henry has used five characters in his story namely Sue, Johnsy, Mr Behrman, the doctor and the janitor where even if the janitor had not played any role directly in the context of the play, his mention in the narrative brings out the fact that it was he who had found Mr Behrman sick and informed the doctor about him, which makes him an important character in the story.

questions.question: What kind of friendship do you think has been portrayed in the story, The Last Leaf?
questions.explanation: In the story, Henry has portrayed an example of true friendship where a friend looks after another friend without demanding anything in return. Sue looks after Johnsy selflessly with only one motive in her mind, to make Johnsy feel better and recover from her illness.

questions.question: Who is the author of the chapter, A House is Not a Home?
questions.explanation: The author of the chapter, A House is Not a Home is Zan Gaudioso. She is a well-known writer, who is deeply involved in her writings. She has created some brilliant masterpieces in the literature field.

questions.question: The author narrated the story about which of the age group? In reference to the chapter, A House is Not a Home.
questions.explanation: The author narrated her real story about her teenage life. She reflected the challenges of being a teenager and the problems of growing up.

questions.question: What do you think about the plot of the chapter, A House is not a Home?

questions.question: Who is the author of the chapter, The Beggar?
questions.explanation: The author of the chapter is Anton Chekhov. He is a very famous writer who is well known for his writing skills. He used to create his creation in a logical manner.

questions.question: Who is the Protagonist of the chapter, The Beggar?
questions.explanation: The Protagonist means the main character of the chapter and the main character of the chapter is Lushkoff as the whole story revolves around the life of him who was a beggar in the beginning and soon turned into a civilized man.

questions.question: How many characters are there in the chapter, The Beggar?
questions.explanation: There are a total of three characters in the chapter, who are named as Sergei, Lushkoff and Olga respectively. All the characters are somewhat related to the chapter but the character of Lushkoff is the main amongst them.

questions.question: What did Lushkoff tell to Sergei about his profession in the past eight years in the village?
questions.explanation: Lushkoff was a beggar in reality but he lied about his profession to Sergei. He articulated that he was a school teacher for the past eight years in the village.

questions.question:
Who is he in the below line? (The Beggar|)

This is dishonesty, my dear sir! he cried angrily.
questions.explanation: In the above line, he here refers to Sergei. This is a dialogue which is delivered by Sergei in a conversation between him and Lushkoff.

questions.question: Which work was allowed to Lushkoff by Sergei in his house?
questions.explanation: Lushkoff was allowed to work in Sergei's house. He was assigned a work to chop wood in the house. Lushkoff accepted the proposal and started working on it.

questions.question: Why did the beggar turn into a woodcutter? (The Beggar)

options.option: He was trapped in his own words

questions.explanation: The Beggar that is Lushkoff was turned into a woodcutter as he was trapped in his own words. Categorically, he accepted the proposal of Sergei for chopping wood in his house, but later on, he discovered that he was not meant for that job but he continued working because he was trapped in his own words.

questions.question: What was Olga by her profession?
questions.explanation: Olga was a cook by her profession. She used to work for Sergei and later on helped Lushkoff also for completing his work. She was an enthusiastic woman who was well known for her work.

questions.question: What was Sergei by his profession?
questions.explanation: Sergei was an advocate by his profession. He was a good human being. He hired Olga as his cook and he also helped Lushkoff to get him to work.

questions.question: Why did Olga begin to rebuke Lushkoff? (The Beggar)

options.option: He was not doing his work properly
He was not helping her in the work

questions.explanation: Olga was a companion of Lushkoff and she wanted his companion to do his job properly. But, Lushkoff was not doing his work properly so Olga was a bit frustrated and in her frustration, she started rebuking him.

questions.question: How much amount was being paid to Lushkoff for wood chopping work?
questions.explanation: Lushkoff was assigned work of chopping wood by Sergei. He was a source of income for him and he got around a half Rouble for the desired work.

questions.question:
What is the meaning of copecks in the given line? (The Beggar)
Every time he received from twenty to forty copecks.
questions.explanation: The word copecks is used to denote Russian Coins in which its denomination is equal to the one-hundredth of a Rouble.

questions.question:
Who is being addressed as a waif in the given line?
This time the waif was sober,
questions.explanation: In reference to the context, Lushkoff was addressed as a waif here. Categorically; waif means a helpless or a homeless person.

questions.question: How much amount was paid to Lushkoff for helping Sergei in his packing work?
questions.explanation: Apart from assigning Lushkoff the work of wood chopping, Sergei also assigned him a work of packing for which he was paid one Rouble.

questions.question: What was the monthly income of Lushkoff after becoming a notary?
questions.explanation: From starting life as a beggar, Lushkoff ended it up on a very good note by becoming a well-civilized man who was a notary and had a monthly income of around thirty-five Roubles.

questions.question:
Who said these lines to whom? (The Beggar)

If I hadn't come to you then I might still have been calling myself a teacher or a student.
questions.explanation: This is a short extract taken from a conversation between Sergei and Lushkoff in which these lines are said by Lushkoff to Sergei.

questions.question:
Who was addressed as a sot and miserable creature in reference to the chapter, The Beggar?
Oh, you sot, you! Oh, you miserable creature!
questions.explanation: Lushkoff was addressed as a sot and miserable creature. Categorically sot means an addictive person who is a habitual drunkard.

questions.question: What do you think about the irony of the chapter, The Beggar?

options.option: Olga was an actual person behind the Lushkoff's success

questions.explanation: The Irony of the chapter is somewhat a very unusual thing which has happened and here the most unusual thing happened was the role reversal. Here Sergei gave Lushkoff a platform to enhance his standard of living but the irony was Olga was the actual person behind his all success.
